era_context: Late 15th century: Iberian Reconquista accelerating toward conclusion; Portuguese maritime exploration advancing southward along African coast; Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan and Mediterranean dominance; Ming Dynasty at height of centralized power; Aztec Empire under Ahuitzotl; African kingdoms engaged in expanding Atlantic trade; European Renaissance flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; oral traditions underrepresented)

events.title: Granada War continues; Christian forces advance toward final victory
events.description: The Granada War (1482–1492) continues with Christian forces under Ferdinand and Isabella maintaining military pressure on the Nasrid Emirate. By 1487, the Christian advance is accelerating, with key Nasrid strongholds falling to Christian forces. The Nasrid ruler Muhammad XII (Boabdil) is increasingly isolated and pressured to negotiate. This year represents a critical phase in the final Reconquista, with Christian victory appearing inevitable. The war's conclusion will mark the end of Islamic political power in Iberia and presage the expulsion of Muslims and Jews.

sources.name: Andrés Bernáldez, Historia de los Reyes Católicos
Pulgar, Crónica de los Reyes Católicos

events.certainty_note: The Granada War's progression is well-documented in Spanish chronicles. The year 1487 is attested as a period of Christian military advance.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration continues; expeditions probe West African coast
events.description: Portuguese navigators continue systematic exploration of the West African coast under the patronage of João II. These expeditions serve both commercial (slave trade, gold, spices) and strategic purposes (establishing trading posts, gathering geographical knowledge). By 1487, Portuguese traders have established a network of coastal forts and trading posts, and are beginning to probe further south toward the Cape of Good Hope. The exploration is driven by commercial interest and the Portuguese crown's ambition to monopolize Atlantic trade and find a sea route to Asia.

sources.name: Portuguese royal charters and commercial records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese maritime exploration in this period is well-documented in Portuguese archives and contemporary accounts.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian tensions escalate; naval conflicts in Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Ottoman forces under Bayezid II continue to challenge Venetian naval and commercial dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean. By 1487, Ottoman military power is clearly superior to Venice's, and tensions are escalating. Ottoman corsairs and naval forces are increasingly active, threatening Venetian trade routes and colonial possessions. These tensions will culminate in the Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War (1499–1503), which will result in significant Venetian territorial losses.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and Turkish chronicles

events.certainty_note: Ottoman-Venetian tensions in this period are well-documented. Specific events in 1487 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty maintains centralized control; administrative and cultural institutions flourish
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor's successors maintains centralized control and continues to support administrative, cultural, and architectural institutions. By 1487, the Ming state represents one of the world's most sophisticated political systems, with a highly developed bureaucracy, extensive infrastructure, and significant cultural achievements. The imperial examination system produces a highly educated elite. Ming ceramics, painting, and literature continue to reach high levels of achievement. This period represents the height of Ming stability and cultural flourishing.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) or his successors
events.certainty_note: Ming administrative and cultural achievements are well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Ahuitzotl becomes Aztec Emperor; begins reign of territorial expansion
events.description: Ahuitzotl (r. 1486–1502) becomes the eighth Aztec emperor, succeeding Tizoc. Ahuitzotl's reign is marked by aggressive military expansion and consolidation of tributary networks. He initiates extensive military campaigns to expand Aztec territory and increase tribute collection. Ahuitzotl is also known for his patronage of monumental architecture, including the expansion of the Templo Mayor. His reign represents the apex of Aztec imperial power and territorial expansion, with the empire reaching its maximum extent by the early 1490s.

sources.name: Durán, Historia de las Indias de Nueva España

events.certainty_note: Ahuitzotl's accession to the throne in 1486 is well-attested in Aztec codices and Spanish colonial accounts. The date 1487 falls within the early years of his reign.

events.title: Italian Renaissance continues; major artistic and intellectual achievements
events.description: The Italian Renaissance continues to flourish, with major artists and intellectuals producing significant works. Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, and other Renaissance figures are at the height of their creative powers. Patronage from wealthy families and the Church supports extensive artistic and architectural projects. By 1487, the Renaissance represents a cultural transformation in Europe, with renewed interest in classical learning, humanism, and naturalistic representation. This cultural movement will have profound long-term effects on European intellectual and artistic traditions.
events.certainty_note: Renaissance artistic achievements are well-documented in surviving artworks and manuscripts.

disconfirming_evidence: The year 1487 is not marked by singular transformative events in most regions; it represents continuity within broader historical processes. The significance attributed to 1487 is partly retrospective, emphasizing the prelude to 1492 and the 'Age of Exploration.' Modern scholarship has questioned the 'Renaissance' as a unified movement and has critiqued the 'Age of Exploration' narrative for erasing non-European agency. Additionally, the characterization of Portuguese exploration as 'discovery' has been challenged for ignoring the knowledge and agency of African and Asian peoples. The dating of Ahuitzotl's accession is sometimes given as 1486 or 1487; sources vary slightly.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1487 is rich for European, Ottoman, and Ming sources. African and Mesoamerican sources are mediated through later European compilation or oral tradition. The historical record privileges political, military, and cultural events. Gaps exist in documentation of economic, social, and environmental changes, and in non-elite perspectives. European-centric bias is evident in the emphasis on Reconquista and Renaissance events. The year's significance is partly constructed by later historians emphasizing the prelude to 1492 and the 'Age of Exploration.'

graph_edges.note: Portuguese exploration continues toward Cape of Good Hope
Ottoman-Venetian tensions escalate into formal war
Ming Dynasty stability and cultural flourishing
Ahuitzotl's reign leads to Montezuma II's succession
